title: *visual mode* Tricks
subtitle: Block {key:O} for diagonal corners. {key:gv} to re-select.
summary: Two tricks that make *visual mode* much more useful: O in block mode swaps to the opposite diagonal corner, and gv re-selects the previous selection.

blocks.text: Two small tricks save lots of pain in *visual mode*: {key:O} (capital O) for moving across the diagonal of a block, and {key:g}{key:v} for getting back the selection you just lost.

In char/line Visual, {key:o} swaps which end of the selection your motions move. In *block* Visual, {key:o} swaps along one diagonal — but {key:O} (capital) swaps to the *opposite* corner of the rectangle. So you can extend the block from any of the four corners without restarting.

blocks.label: (in Block Visual) Move cursor to opposite corner of rectangle

blocks.text: Pressed Esc by accident? Did one operation and want to do another on the same range? {key:g}{key:v} restores the last visual selection — same anchor, same cursor, same mode.

blocks.title: How gv Works
blocks.text:
Whenever you leave *visual mode*, Vim writes two marks ({key:`}{key:<}, {key:`}{key:>}) and remembers the *type* of the selection. {key:g}{key:v} re-enters that type of Visual with the cursor and anchor restored from those marks.

Because it's just two marks, anything that updates them updates {key:g}{key:v}'s target. {key::}{key:s} commands run on the visual range update them implicitly — so you can {key::}{key:'}{key:<}{key:,}{key:'}{key:>}{key:s}{key:/}{key:foo}{key:/}{key:bar}{key:/}{key:g} and then {key:g}{key:v} to grab the same range again.

The range works *per buffer*. Open a different buffer; come back; {key:g}{key:v} still works.

rows: Move to opposite corner (block visual only)

blocks.title: {key:o} swaps the anchor, not the text
blocks.text: In *visual mode*, {key:o} (and {key:O} for visual-block) flips which endpoint is the anchor and which is the cursor. The selection contents don't change — only which end your motions extend. After an operator runs, the cursor lands on whichever endpoint was current at consume time, so swapping ends before {key:d} or {key:y} changes where you end up.
